function.name: patch
function.description:
Targeted find-and-replace edits in files. Use this instead of sed/awk in terminal. Uses fuzzy matching (9 strategies) so minor whitespace/indentation differences won't break it. Returns a unified diff. Auto-runs syntax checks after editing.

REPLACE MODE (mode='replace', default): find a unique string and replace it. REQUIRED PARAMETERS: mode, path, old_string, new_string.
PATCH MODE (mode='patch'): apply V4A multi-file patches for bulk changes. REQUIRED PARAMETERS: mode, patch.

mode.description: Edit mode. 'replace' (default): requires path + old_string + new_string. 'patch': requires patch content only.

path.description: REQUIRED when mode='replace'. File path to edit.

old_string.description: REQUIRED when mode='replace'. Exact text to find and replace. Must be unique in the file unless replace_all=true. Include surrounding context lines to ensure uniqueness.

new_string.description: REQUIRED when mode='replace'. Replacement text. Pass empty string '' to delete the matched text.

replace_all.description: Replace all occurrences instead of requiring a unique match (default: false)

patch.description:
REQUIRED when mode='patch'. V4A format patch content. Format:
*** Begin Patch
*** Update File: path/to/file
@@ context hint @@
 context line
-removed line
+added line
*** End Patch

function.name: read_file
function.description: Read a text file with line numbers and pagination. Use this instead of cat/head/tail in terminal. Output format: 'LINE_NUM|CONTENT'. Suggests similar filenames if not found. Use offset and limit for large files. Reads exceeding ~100K characters are rejected; use offset and limit to read specific sections of large files. NOTE: Cannot read images or binary files — use vision_analyze for images.

path.description: Path to the file to read (absolute, relative, or ~/path)

offset.description: Line number to start reading from (1-indexed, default: 1)

limit.description: Maximum number of lines to read (default: 500, max: 2000)

function.name: search_files
function.description:
Search file contents or find files by name. Use this instead of grep/rg/find/ls in terminal. Ripgrep-backed, faster than shell equivalents.

Content search (target='content'): Regex search inside files. Output modes: full matches with line numbers, file paths only, or match counts.

File search (target='files'): Find files by glob pattern (e.g., '*.py', '*config*'). Also use this instead of ls — results sorted by modification time.

pattern.description: Regex pattern for content search, or glob pattern (e.g., '*.py') for file search

target.description: 'content' searches inside file contents, 'files' searches for files by name

path.description: Directory or file to search in (default: current working directory)

file_glob.description: Filter files by pattern in grep mode (e.g., '*.py' to only search Python files)

limit.description: Maximum number of results to return (default: 50)

offset.description: Skip first N results for pagination (default: 0)

output_mode.description: Output format for grep mode: 'content' shows matching lines with line numbers, 'files_only' lists file paths, 'count' shows match counts per file

context.description: Number of context lines before and after each match (grep mode only)

function.name: write_file
function.description: Write content to a file, completely replacing existing content. Use this instead of echo/cat heredoc in terminal. Creates parent directories automatically. OVERWRITES the entire file — use 'patch' for targeted edits. Auto-runs syntax checks on .py/.json/.yaml/.toml and other linted languages; only NEW errors introduced by this write are surfaced (pre-existing errors are filtered out).

path.description: Path to the file to write (will be created if it doesn't exist, overwritten if it does)

content.description: Complete content to write to the file

messages.name: read_file
messages.content:
{"content": "     1|---
     2|name: api-testing
     3|description: API integration testing with SuperTest and contract testing
     4|author: Charmander
     5|tags: [testing, api, integration]
     6|team: charmander
     7|---
     8|
     9|# api-testing
    10|
    11|API integration testing skill for Charmander QA. Covers REST endpoint testing, authentication/authorization validation, error handling, contract testing, and the associated tooling ecosystem.
    12|
    13|## Tooling
    14|
    15|### SuperTest (Primary)
    16|
    17|- **Purpose**: HTTP assertion library built on top of SuperAgent for testing Node.js HTTP servers.
    18|- **Usage Pattern**:
    19|  ```js
    20|  const request = require('supertest');
    21|  const app = require('../app');
    22|
    23|  describe('GET /api/users', () => {
    24|    it('returns a list of users', async () => {
    25|      const res = await request(app)
    26|        .get('/api/users')
    27|        .set('Authorization', `Bearer ${token}`)
    28|        .expect(200);
    29|      expect(res.body).toHaveProperty('users');
    30|      expect(Array.isArray(res.body.users)).toBe(true);
    31|    });
    32|  });
    33|  ```
    34|- **Best Practices**:
    35|  - Chain `.expect()` for status code assertions.
    36|  - Use `async/await` for readability.
    37|  - Always pass the app instance (not a running server) to keep tests isolated.
    38|  - Use `.set()` for headers, `.send()` for request bodies.
    39|
    40|### Postman / Newman
    41|
    42|- **Purpose**: Collection-based API testing with Newman for CI/CD execution.
    43|- **Usage**:
    44|  ```bash
    45|  newman run collection.json \
    46|    --environment env.json \
    47|    --reporters cli,json \
    48|    --reporter-json-export results.json
    49|  ```
    50|- **Best Practices**:
    51|  - Store collections and environments under version control.
    52|  - Use Postman variables (e.g., `{{baseUrl}}`, `{{authToken}}`) for environment agnosticism.
    53|  - Write Pre-request Scripts for dynamic setup (e.g., token refresh).
    54|  - Write Test Scripts (Postman Sandbox) for response validation:
    55|    ```js
    56|    pm.test('Status code is 200', () => pm.response.to.have.status(200));
    57|    pm.test('Response has data', () => pm.response.to.have.jsonBody('data'));
    58|    ```
    59|- **CI Integration**: Run Newman via Docker or direct CLI in pipeline steps.
    60|
    61|### Pact (Contract Testing)
    62|
    63|- **Purpose**: Consumer-driven contract testing between services.
    64|- **Components**:
    65|  - **Pact Broker**: Shares contracts between consumer and provider.
    66|  - **Pact CLI Tools**: `pact-broker`, `pact-provider-verifier`.
    67|- **Consumer (Expectation Setup)**:
    68|  ```js
    69|  const { Pact } = require('@pact-foundation/pact');
    70|
    71|  const provider = new Pact({
    72|    consumer: 'WebApp',
    73|    provider: 'UserService',
    74|    port: 1234,
    75|  });
    76|
    77|  beforeAll(() =>
    78|    provider.setup().then(() => {
    79|      provider.addInteraction({
    80|        state: 'a user exists',
    81|        uponReceiving: 'a request for a user',
    82|        withRequest: {
    83|          method: 'GET',
    84|          path: '/users/1',
    85|        },
    86|        willRespondWith: {
    87|          status: 200,
    88|          headers: { 'Content-Type': 'application/json' },
    89|          body: { id: 1, name: like('Jane') },
    90|        },
    91|      });
    92|    })
    93|  );
    94|  ```
    95|- **Provider (Verification)**:
    96|  ```js
    97|  const { Verifier } = require('@pact-foundation/pact');
    98|
    99|  new Verifier({
   100|    providerBaseUrl: 'http://localhost:3000',
   101|    pactBrokerUrl: 'http://localhost:9292',
   102|    provider: 'UserService',
   103|    publishVerificationResult: true,
   104|  }).verifyProvider();
   105|  ```
   106|
   107|## Test Patterns for REST Endpoints
   108|
   109|### Standard CRUD Pattern
   110|
   111|```
   112|POST   /api/resources      → 201 Created (create)
   113|GET    /api/resources      → 200 OK (list)
   114|GET    /api/resources/:id  → 200 OK (retrieve)
   115|PUT    /api/resources/:id  → 200 OK (full update)
   116|PATCH  /api/resources/:id  → 200 OK (partial update)
   117|DELETE /api/resources/:id  → 204 No Content (delete)
   118|```
   119|
   120|### Test Structure Template
   121|
   122|```js
   123|describe('Resource API', () => {
   124|  let authToken;
   125|
   126|  beforeAll(async () => {
   127|    authToken = await getAuthToken();
   128|  });
   129|
   130|  describe('POST /api/resources', () => {
   131|    it('creates a new resource with valid payload', async () => { /* ... */ });
   132|    it('returns 400 for missing required fields', async () => { /* ... */ });
   133|    it('returns 401 without auth token', async () => { /* ... */ });
   134|  });
   135|
   136|  describe('GET /api/resources/:id', () => {
   137|    it('returns the resource by ID', async () => { /* ... */ });
   138|    it('returns 404 for non-existent ID', async () => { /* ... */ });
   139|  });
   140|});
   141|```
   142|
   143|### Idempotency & Side Effects
   144|
   145|- Test that `PUT` is idempotent (same payload, same result).
   146|- Test that `DELETE` returns 410 on subsequent calls (or 404 depending on API design).
   147|- Verify read endpoints have no side effects (no state mutations).
   148|
   149|## Auth Testing
   150|
   151|### Token-Based Auth (JWT / Bearer)
   152|
   153|- **Positive**: Valid token → 2xx.
   154|- **Negative**: Missing token → 401 Unauthorized.
   155|- **Negative**: Expired token → 401 Unauthorized.
   156|- **Negative**: Malformed token → 400/401.
   157|- **Negative**: Revoked token → 401/403.
   158|
   159|```js
   160|it('rejects requests without auth header', async () => {
   161|  await request(app)
   162|    .get('/api/protected')
   163|    .expect(401);
   164|});
   165|
   166|it('rejects expired tokens', async () => {
   167|  const expired = generateExpiredToken();
   168|  await request(app)
   169|    .get('/api/protected')
   170|    .set('Authorization', `Bearer ${expired}`)
   171|    .expect(401);
   172|});
   173|```
   174|
   175|### API Key Auth
   176|
   177|- **Header-based**: `X-API-Key`.
   178|- **Query-based**: `?api_key=...`.
   179|- **Negative tests**: Missing key, invalid key, revoked key.
   180|
   181|### Role-Based Access Control (RBAC)
   182|
   183|- Test that each role can only access authorized endpoints.
   184|- Test that unauthorized roles receive 403 Forbidden.
   185|
   186|```js
   187|it('admin can delete users', async () => { /* 200/204 with admin token */ });
   188|it('viewer cannot delete users', async () => { /* 403 with viewer token */ });
   189|```
   190|
   191|## Error Handling
   192|
   193|### Standard Error Response Shape
   194|
   195|Verify the API returns a consistent error envelope:
   196|
   197|```json
   198|{
   199|  "error": {
   200|    "status": 400,
   201|    "code": "VALIDATION_ERROR",
   202|    "message": "Field 'email' is required",
   203|    "details": [
   204|      { "field": "email", "issue": "required" }
   205|    ]
   206|  }
   207|}
   208|```
   209|
   210|### Test Matrix for Error Scenarios
   211|
   212|| Scenario                | Status | Pattern                                  |
   213||-------------------------|--------|------------------------------------------|
   214|| Missing body fields     | 400    | Validation error with field-level detail |
   215|| Invalid data types      | 400    | Type mismatch error                      |
   216|| Resource not found      | 404    | Not found error                          |
   217|| Unauthenticated         | 401    | Auth error message                       |
   218|| Forbidden               | 403    | Permission error                         |
   219|| Conflict (duplicate)    | 409    | Conflict error with identifier           |
   220|| Rate limited            | 429    | Retry-After header                       |
   221|| Internal server error   | 500    | Generic error, no stack trace leak       |
   222|| Unprocessable entity    | 422    | Semantic validation failure              |
   223|
   224|### Security Checks in Error Responses
   225|
   226|- **No stack traces** leaked in production error responses.
   227|- **No SQL/database details** exposed.
   228|- **Consistent error schema** across all endpoints.
   229|
   230|```js
   231|it('does not leak stack traces on 500', async () => {
   232|  const res = await request(app)
   233|    .get('/api/trigger-error')
   234|    .expect(500);
   235|  expect(res.body.error).not.toHaveProperty('stack');
   236|  expect(res.body.error).not.toHaveProperty('trace');
   237|});
   238|```
   239|
   240|## Contract Testing Approach
   241|
   242|### Workflow
   243|
   244|1. **Consumer writes expectations** using Pact DSL in unit tests.
   245|2. **Pact Broker** stores the generated contract (`pacts/consumer-provider.json`).
   246|3. **Provider verifies** against the contract using `pact-provider-verifier`.
   247|4. **Results published** back to Pact Broker.
   248|5. **Matrix view** in Pact Broker shows which consumer versions are compatible with which provider versions.
   249|
   250|### Can-I-Deploy Check
   251|
   252|Use the Pact Broker's `can-i-deploy` tool to gate deployments:
   253|
   254|```bash
   255|pact-broker can-i-deploy \
   256|  --pacticipant UserService \
   257|  --version 1.2.3 \
   258|  --to-environment production
   259|```
   260|
   261|### Contract Best Practices
   262|
   263|- Keep contracts small — test real, specific interactions, not every possible response.
   264|- Use Pact matchers (`like()`, `term()`, `eachLike()`) instead of exact values for flexible verification.
   265|- Version both consumer and provider contracts.
   266|- Run provider verification as part of CI on every merge to main.
   267|- Tag contracts with environment names (`prod`, `staging`) for deployment gating.
   268|
   269|### Example Pact Matchers
   270|
   271|```js
   272|const { like, term, eachLike } = require('@pact-foundation/pact');
   273|
   274|body: {
   275|  id: like(1),
   276|  name: like('Jane'),
   277|  email: term({ generate: 'jane@example.com', matcher: '\\S+@\\S+\\.\\S+' }),
   278|  roles: eachLike('admin'),
   279|}
   280|```
   281|
   282|## Directory Layout
   283|
   284|```
   285|api-tests/
   286|├── collections/          # Postman collections
   287|│   ├── users.postman_collection.json
   288|│   └── auth.postman_collection.json
   289|├── environments/         # Postman environment files
   290|│   ├── dev.postman_environment.json
   291|│   └── staging.postman_environment.json
   292|├── contracts/            # Pact contract definitions
   293|│   ├── consumers/
   294|│   └── providers/
   295|├── supertest/            # SuperTest test files
   296|│   ├── users.test.js
   297|│   ├── auth.test.js
   298|│   └── helpers/
   299|│       ├── auth.js       # Token generation helpers
   300|│       └── fixtures.js   # Test data factories
   301|└── reports/              # Test output and reports
   302|    ├── pact/
   303|    └── newman/
   304|```
   305|
   306|## CI Integration
   307|
   308|```yaml
   309|# Example GitHub Actions workflow step
   310|- name: Run API Contract Tests
   311|  run: |
   312|    cd api-tests
   313|    npx jest --ci --reporters=default --reporters=jest-junit
   314|    npx pact-broker can-i-deploy \
   315|      --pacticipant WebApp \
   316|      --version ${{ github.sha }} \
   317|      --to-environment staging
   318|```
   319|", "total_lines": 318, "file_size": 9699, "truncated": false, "is_binary": false, "is_image": false}
